title: M4R9H: Harden automated proof for compiled fast-path execution and warm reuse
description:
## Problem Statement
Compiled-fast-path work is especially vulnerable to silent regression. It is easy for a branch to keep producing correct token outputs while secretly drifting back to host loops, eager host extraction, or one-compile-per-page behavior. Output-only tests will not reliably catch that. The repo therefore needs a dedicated hardening bead for the compiled fast path, analogous to the earlier M4 guardrail work for fallback CPU regressions.

## Scope
Harden the automated verification suite so the repo fails immediately if the compiled fast path regresses to host-side candidate generation, mid-pipeline host extraction, or unstable compile/cache behavior. This bead owns the new proof surfaces, machine-readable backend markers, and verification-lane enforcement for the compiled fast path.

## Concrete Work Items
- Audit the existing tensor-core, CLI, smoke, and verification suites for places where the compiled fast path could regress without failing tests.
- Add machine-readable backend markers or counters that distinguish compiled-device execution from host fallback behavior for the fast families.
- Add assertions that detect forbidden mid-pipeline host extraction before final packed rows.
- Add assertions that detect per-page recompilation churn when the same bucket and runtime config are reused.
- Ensure the canonical verification lane exercises at least one literal-heavy, one run-heavy, and one prefixed-heavy workload through the compiled fast path.
- Extend CLI or smoke output only as needed to make the proof machine-readable and testable.
- Remove or rewrite any test that still passes if the fast path secretly falls back to host-array execution.

## Likely File Targets
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LexPageTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LiteralExecutionTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/CandidateVerificationTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/KernelCacheTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/GoldenTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftCLITests/*` if smoke output must be asserted
- `Scripts/dev`
- `Scripts/verify`
- the MLX smoke/proof command if new machine-readable fields are required

## Non-Goals
- Do not rely on manual profiler inspection, screenshots, or ad hoc debug logs.
- Do not add tests that merely prove helper functions work while the production path remains unverified.
- Do not accept proof that only covers one fast family while the others can silently regress.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The automated suite fails if any fast family regresses to host-side production execution.
- The automated suite fails if the page pipeline extracts intermediate fast-path data to host memory before final packed rows.
- The automated suite fails if repeated same-bucket runs trigger per-page recompilation instead of warm reuse.
- The repo has a canonical machine-readable proof path for compiled fast-path execution that requires no human interpretation.
- Literal-heavy, run-heavy, and prefixed-heavy workloads are all covered by automated verification.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when all of the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. `./dev verify`
3. The repo's MLX smoke/proof command, or its successor, proving compiled fast-path execution and warm reuse through machine-readable output
4. CLI or snapshot tests covering any new proof fields
5. Targeted regression suites under `swift test` that fail on host execution, early extraction, or compile-count regressions

## Closure Guardrail
Do not close this bead if a developer still has to inspect free-form logs to decide whether the compiled fast path really ran. The proof story must be fully automated and machine-readable.
notes: IMPORTANT BUILD NOTE: Metal shaders cannot be compiled via swift package manager (swift build/swift test). Any tests that exercise MLX tensor ops requiring GPU shaders MUST be run with xcodebuild (e.g. xcodebuild test -scheme MicroSwift -destination 'platform=macOS'). Use swift build and swift test only for compilation checks and non-GPU tests. Design code so that the core logic is testable without GPU, but integration/smoke tests that touch real MLX execution should be validated via xcodebuild.

title: M4R9G: Compile and cache per-bucket MLX fast-path graphs
description:
## Problem Statement
The architectural payoff of the migration does not arrive merely because the page pipeline uses MLX operations. If the runtime still rebuilds the graph or recompiles kernels for every page, warm-path throughput will stay poor and the bucket system will fail its main purpose. The new plan depends on `MLX.compile` and deterministic graph caching keyed by stable page shapes and runtime configuration.

## Scope
Wrap the finished device-resident fast-path page pipeline in compiled MLX closures and cache those compiled resources per bucket/runtime configuration. This bead owns the compile boundary, cache-key design, warm-path reuse, and machine-readable metrics that prove the graph is not recompiling on every page.

## Concrete Work Items
- Identify the exact compiled boundary for the fast path, ideally from bucket-padded page bytes and page metadata through final packed rows or the nearest equivalent explicit transport boundary.
- Introduce per-bucket compiled closures using `MLX.compile` or the repo's pragmatic compiled-wrapper equivalent if MLX's exact API shape differs.
- Extend the existing cache story so entries represent real compiled fast-path resources rather than only helper objects.
- Define deterministic cache keys that cover the dimensions that genuinely affect compilation, such as device identity, artifact/runtime hash, page bucket, and any dtype/layout contract that changes the graph shape.
- Ensure repeated execution of pages in the same bucket reuses the compiled closure without recompilation.
- Expose machine-readable compile-count, cache-hit, and cache-miss metrics that automated tests can assert.
- Keep the boundary honest: the compiled graph must encompass the meaningful fast-path work, not only a tiny helper around one primitive.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/KernelCache.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPageAPI.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/FallbackBenchmark.swift` or a sibling benchmark/observability file if cache metrics are shared
- any new compiled-graph wrapper surface introduced for the fast path
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/KernelCacheTests.swift`
- observability or benchmark JSON tests under `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests`
- CLI or smoke command files only if they need to surface new machine-readable compile metrics

## Non-Goals
- Do not compile only a trivial helper and call that the page graph.
- Do not let the cache key depend on page contents if the bucket shape and runtime config are identical.
- Do not accept warm-path claims that require a human to read logs or mentally compare timestamps.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The production fast path executes through a compiled per-bucket MLX graph or an equivalent compiled wrapper that covers the meaningful page pipeline.
- Repeated pages in the same bucket reuse compiled resources instead of recompiling for every invocation.
- Cache keys are deterministic and based on stable runtime dimensions rather than page content.
- The runtime exposes machine-readable compile and cache metrics suitable for automated assertions.
- The compiled boundary is broad enough that it materially represents the migrated fast path, not just one tiny subroutine.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when all of the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/KernelCacheTests.swift`
3. Any new compile-count or cache-metric assertion suites added under `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests`
4. `./dev verify`
5. The repo's MLX smoke/proof command, or its successor, proving via machine-readable output that warm-path reuse occurred for repeated same-bucket execution without manual log reading

## Closure Guardrail
Do not close this bead if the fast path still recompiles on repeated same-bucket pages or if proof of reuse depends on a manual benchmark run. The cache and compile behavior must be assertable automatically.
notes: IMPORTANT BUILD NOTE: Metal shaders cannot be compiled via swift package manager (swift build/swift test). Any tests that exercise MLX tensor ops requiring GPU shaders MUST be run with xcodebuild (e.g. xcodebuild test -scheme MicroSwift -destination 'platform=macOS'). Use swift build and swift test only for compilation checks and non-GPU tests. Design code so that the core logic is testable without GPU, but integration/smoke tests that touch real MLX execution should be validated via xcodebuild.

M4R9: Migrate the fast-path lexer into compiled MLX page graphs
description:
## Objective
The repository now has the first-wave M4 remediation work checked in, but the fast-path lexer still fundamentally behaves like host-array code wrapped in MLX-adjacent types. `LexPageAPI` still assembles rule candidates from host collections, the family executors still reason over page bytes with scalar loops, and `WinnerReduction` still merges candidates on the CPU. That means the host is still doing the expensive byte-by-byte work while Metal is used only for the fallback DFA path.

This epic tracks the second-wave remediation needed to turn the fast-path lexer into an actual compiled MLX page pipeline so bucketed pages stay resident on device from padded ingestion through packed-row transport.

## Why This Exists Separately From The First M4 Remediation Wave
The closed `M4R1` through `M4R8` beads restored the real page-oriented runtime, MLX-backed runtime constants, paging, fallback-kernel proof lanes, and test hardening needed to make Milestone 4 honest. That work was necessary, but it does not by itself deliver the architecture described in the new migration strategy:
- one padded device upload per page bucket
- literal/run/prefixed fast-family execution as MLX tensor math rather than host loops
- fused rule-axis reduction instead of host candidate arrays
- device-resident packed-row construction
- `MLX.compile` warm-path reuse keyed by `PageBucket`

Without this epic, the branch can still satisfy the fallback milestone while leaving the high-throughput fast path mostly on the CPU.

## Desired End State
After the child beads in this epic are complete, the production fast path must satisfy all of the following conditions:
1. Each `SourcePage` is padded to an exact `PageBucket.byteCapacity` and uploaded once as a shape-stable `MLXArray` of bytes.
2. Byte classification, class-set membership, literal matching, run-family execution, prefixed execution, winner reduction, selection, remap, coverage, lexical-error spans, and packed-row assembly remain device-resident until an explicit extraction boundary.
3. The fast path no longer materializes `[UInt8]`, `[UInt16]`, or `[WinnerReduction.RuleCandidate]` as canonical production-path storage inside the hot loop.
4. The runtime exposes a compiled per-bucket MLX graph that can be cached and reused without per-page recompilation churn.
5. The verification lane fails automatically if the compiled fast path silently regresses to host loops, eager host extraction, or one-compile-per-page behavior.

## Dependency Graph Intent
The child beads are deliberately split to preserve parallelism while respecting the real architecture:
- one device-page-ingestion bead establishes the shape-stable page contract and the no-early-egress rule
- three family-migration beads can then proceed in parallel for `literal`, `classRun/headTail`, and `prefixed`
- one reduction bead fuses those outputs into device winner tensors
- one post-reduction bead keeps selection, remap, coverage, and packed-row assembly on device
- one compiled-graph bead wraps the finished page pipeline in `MLX.compile` and caches it per bucket
- one hardening bead makes regressions fail automatically in tests and the canonical verification lane

## Global Acceptance Criteria
- The production fast-path lexer executes as a compiled MLX page pipeline rather than a collection of host loops that merely call into MLX at the edges.
- Fast-family candidate generation stays on device for `literal`, `classRun`, `headTail`, and `prefixed`.
- Rule reduction and packed-row construction no longer depend on host candidate arrays in the production path.
- Repeated pages in the same bucket reuse compiled graphs instead of recompiling because of shape churn.
- The automated proof lane can detect host regressions without manual profiler inspection or handwritten log interpretation.

## Global Automated Proof Plan
The epic is only closable when the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. `./dev verify`
3. The repo's MLX smoke/proof command, or its successor, proving the compiled fast path executed through the intended backend using machine-readable output
4. Any targeted compiled-fast-path differential suites added by the child beads under `swift test`

No closure evidence may rely on manual Instruments sessions, ad hoc print statements, screenshots, or human interpretation of free-form logs.

M4R9F: Keep selection, remap, coverage, and packed-row assembly on device
description:
## Problem Statement
Moving family execution and rule reduction onto the device is necessary but still incomplete. The current page pipeline also performs greedy selection, keyword remap, coverage/error accounting, and packed-row assembly after reduction. If those phases continue to run by extracting winners back to the CPU, the runtime will still bounce across the host/device boundary in the middle of the hot path. That violates the stated migration strategy, which explicitly requires keeping page data resident on device until final packed transport rows are constructed.

## Scope
Extend the compiled fast path beyond winner reduction so the page-local post-processing stages remain device-resident through final packed-row transport. This bead owns the device story for greedy selection or an equivalent deterministic keep-mask computation, keyword remap application, coverage/error-span construction, and final packed-row assembly.

## Concrete Work Items
- Define the device-resident winner/selection data model consumed after the fused reduction bead.
- Port the greedy non-overlap selector to a device-capable formulation. If a small custom kernel is the clearest correct approach for the selector scan, that is in scope here, but the result must stay deterministic and fully automated.
- Apply keyword remap using runtime tables without forcing host extraction of intermediate tokens.
- Keep coverage computation and lexical-error span derivation inside the page pipeline until the final transport boundary.
- Assemble packed token rows as MLX-backed transport data, preserving the current row encoding and deterministic ordering guarantees.
- Provide explicit extraction helpers for shell layers, tests, and snapshots so host inspection happens only at the end of the page pipeline.
- Ensure any page-local masks or span tensors continue to respect `validLen`, page boundaries, and bucket padding.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/GreedySelector.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPageAPI.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/PackedToken.swift` or the equivalent packed-row transport surface
- coverage/remap/error-span helper files used by the page runtime
- any new tensor helper or tiny Metal selector kernel wrapper if needed
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LexPageTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/GoldenTests.swift`
- coverage, keyword-remap, and packed-row tests under `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests`

## Non-Goals
- Do not declare victory after reduction if the production path still extracts winners to host memory before token rows exist.
- Do not change the packed-row wire format or keyword-remap semantics unless the repo already has a deliberate migration plan for that.
- Do not move correctness-critical stages into opaque code that cannot be differential-tested.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The production fast path stays device-resident from bucketed page input through final packed-row assembly.
- Greedy selection, keyword remap, coverage/error derivation, and packed-row construction no longer require mid-pipeline host extraction.
- Final packed rows are exposed through an explicit extraction boundary suitable for shell/test inspection.
- Page-local semantics, token ordering, and packed-row encoding remain deterministic and compatible with existing downstream consumers.
- The resulting page pipeline is suitable to sit inside one compiled per-bucket MLX closure in the next bead.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when all of the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LexPageTests.swift`
3. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/GoldenTests.swift`
4. Keyword-remap, coverage, lexical-error-span, and packed-row tests under `swift test`
5. Multi-page shell or integration suites that prove the final transport boundary still yields correct decoded tokens without any manual inspection

## Closure Guardrail
Do not close this bead if the production path still pulls winner data back to the CPU before final packed rows are built. The device-residence rule must hold through the transport boundary.
notes: IMPORTANT BUILD NOTE: Metal shaders cannot be compiled via swift package manager (swift build/swift test). Any tests that exercise MLX tensor ops requiring GPU shaders MUST be run with xcodebuild (e.g. xcodebuild test -scheme MicroSwift -destination 'platform=macOS'). Use swift build and swift test only for compilation checks and non-GPU tests. Design code so that the core logic is testable without GPU, but integration/smoke tests that touch real MLX execution should be validated via xcodebuild.

M4R5: Replace the ad hoc LexShell pager with the deterministic MicroSwiftFrontend paging contract
description:
## Problem Statement
`Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexShell.swift` currently splits pages by a simplistic `pageCapacity(for:)` calculation and slices the byte array directly. That is not the paging contract described in `history/m4.md`, which requires the runtime to consume the deterministic frontend paging plan from `MicroSwiftFrontend` (or an equivalent replacement with the same observable behavior).

## Scope
Replace the current shell paging logic with the actual frontend page-planning contract so that M4 uses deterministic page boundaries, page-local execution, and structured overflow handling rather than ad hoc fixed-width slicing.

## Concrete Work Items
- Reintroduce or wire up the `MicroSwiftFrontend` dependency needed by `MicroSwiftTensorCore`.
- Replace the current `LexShell` page splitter with `SourcePaging.planPages(...)` / `PreparedSource` or an equivalent surface with the same invariants.
- Preserve deterministic overflow behavior and ensure the shell never falls back to host scalar lexing when a page is unsupported.
- Ensure page-local `validLen` semantics are correct for both fast and fallback rules.
- Make page result padding deterministic with the actual bucket policy rather than merely using lookahead width as page size.
- Ensure base offsets remain correct across multiple pages.

## Likely File Targets
- `Package.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexShell.swift`
- any restored `PagingShell`/overflow support files
- `Sources/MicroSwiftFrontend/*` only if integration hooks need small adaptations
- page-boundary and overflow tests

## Non-Goals
- Do not implement the Metal kernel here.
- Do not use host scalar lexing as a repair strategy for overlong pages.
- Do not relax the page-local execution guarantee by reading across page boundaries.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The shell uses the deterministic frontend paging contract from `MicroSwiftFrontend`, or an equivalent implementation with the same externally visible invariants.
- Page boundaries are deterministic, contiguous, and page-local.
- Overflow behavior is structured and automated; unsupported pages fail with deterministic diagnostics rather than host repair.
- Base offsets and deterministic tail padding are correct across multi-page lexing.
- Existing fallback page-boundary tests and fast-path page-boundary tests both execute through the same shell story.

## Automated Test Plan
All validation must be automated:
1. `swift test`
2. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/PageBoundaryTests.swift`
3. relevant `MicroSwiftFrontend` page-planning suites under `swift test`
4. any multi-page `LexShell` / token-assembly suites added or ported by the foundation bead
5. `./dev verify`

## Closure Guardrail
This bead must not be closed if `LexShell` still pages by raw fixed-length chunking or if overflow correctness depends on a manual spot-check rather than automated assertions.

M4R9E: Replace host candidate arrays with fused MLX winner reduction
description:
## Problem Statement
Even if each fast-family bead produces device-resident candidate lengths, the overall lexer will still bottleneck on the CPU if `WinnerReduction.reduce` continues to allocate and merge host-side candidate arrays. The current reduction surface is built around `[WinnerReduction.RuleCandidate]`, which defeats fusion, increases host allocation churn, and forces the family beads to cross back out of the MLX graph before the real competition between rules even begins.

## Scope
Replace the host candidate-array reduction path with a fused MLX rule-axis reduction over device-resident candidate tensors. This bead owns the canonical tensor layout for rule competition, deterministic tie-breaking across length/priority/rule ID, and device emission of per-position winner fields needed by the selector.

## Concrete Work Items
- Define the shared tensor shape that the family beads must target, such as stacked rule-axis matrices for candidate length and the metadata fields needed for tie-breaking.
- Rework the production reduction path so it consumes device tensors directly rather than `[WinnerReduction.RuleCandidate]` arrays.
- Implement deterministic winner selection that preserves the existing comparison order: longer match first, then smaller `priorityRank`, then smaller `ruleID`.
- Decide whether the tie-break should be expressed as staged boolean masks, encoded composite ranks, or another MLX-friendly representation. The representation must stay readable and testable.
- Ensure invalid positions and padded tail positions are masked to non-winning neutral values.
- Preserve compatibility with fallback winners if the shared pipeline still expects them to join the same reduction story.
- Add explicit host extraction helpers for tests only where needed; the production reducer must remain device-resident.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/WinnerReduction.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPageAPI.swift`
- any new tensor-reduction helper extracted for tie-break logic
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/WinnerReductionTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/ReductionVerificationTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/MixedOverlapTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LexPageTests.swift`

## Non-Goals
- Do not keep the old host reduction as the production path with a new MLX path only used in one benchmark or test.
- Do not weaken deterministic ordering just because a reduction is now expressed in tensor form.
- Do not let family beads return to host memory before reduction merely to match the old API.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The production reducer consumes device-resident candidate tensors from the fast families.
- The production fast path no longer materializes `[WinnerReduction.RuleCandidate]` as the canonical hot-loop reduction input.
- The reducer preserves deterministic ordering by match length, then `priorityRank`, then `ruleID`.

title: M4R8: Harden the automated verification suite so vacuous outputs and host-only regressions cannot pass
description:
## Problem Statement
The current `m4` branch demonstrates how easy it is for a milestone to look “green” while the core deliverable is still missing. Today the branch contains tests that can pass while:
- `LexPage` does not execute real fast-family logic
- compatibility checks compare empty or weak outputs
- the fallback runner still performs the DFA walk on the CPU

This bead closes that loophole.

## Scope
Harden the automated verification suite so the branch cannot regress back to vacuous or host-only behavior without failing tests immediately.

## Concrete Work Items
- Audit the existing `m4` tests for weak assertions that can pass on stubbed behavior.
- Strengthen `V0CompatibilityTests` so they compare meaningful decoded token outputs, spans, diagnostics, and page behavior rather than simple equality of potentially empty results.
- Ensure fallback differential/property tests are explicitly tied to the production MLX/Metal runner and the scalar oracle.
- Add backend markers or machine-readable assertions where needed so tests can detect a CPU regression.
- Ensure the global verification lane (`swift test`, `./dev verify`, smoke path) exercises both:
  - the conservative-extension story for artifacts with no fallback rules
  - the fallback-heavy story for artifacts that do require fallback
- Remove or rewrite any test that only proves a helper function or mock path works while the production runtime remains stubbed.

## Likely File Targets
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/V0CompatibilityTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LexPageTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/FallbackKernelRunnerTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/DifferentialFallbackTests.swift`

## Non-Goals
- Do not add manual QA steps.
- Do not accept benchmark screenshots, profiler traces, or handwritten notes as closure evidence.
- Do not retain weak compatibility tests for the sake of convenience.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The automated test suite fails if `LexPage` reverts to stubbed fast-family behavior.
- The automated test suite fails if the production fallback path silently reverts to CPU stepping.
- `v0` compatibility tests compare meaningful outputs from real lexing rather than vacuous equality.
- The repo has one automated verification story that enforces these guarantees without human interpretation.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when the following all pass automatically:
1. `swift test`
2. `./dev verify`
3. `./dev mlx-smoke` or its successor fallback-proof command
4. any targeted fallback-heavy suites needed to prove scalar-vs-MLX agreement and backend selection under `swift test`

## Closure Guardrail

title: M4 Remediation: deliver a spec-compliant MLX/Metal fallback runtime on top of the real M3 lexer pipeline
description:
## Objective
Bring the current `m4` branch into conformance with the checked-in Milestone 4 spec in `history/m4.md`.

The branch already contains useful fallback-specific work:
- `ArtifactRuntime` fallback table packing
- `CapabilityValidator` support for `v1-fallback`
- `ScalarFallbackEvaluator` as a reference oracle
- differential/property/golden fallback tests
- cache/benchmark/observability scaffolding

But the branch is not yet delivering the actual Milestone 4 runtime promised by the spec:
- `MicroSwiftTensorCore` does not yet depend on `MLX`
- `ArtifactRuntime.byteToClassLUT` is still a host array rather than `MLXArray<UInt16>`
- `PageLexResult.packedRows` is still `[UInt64]` rather than `MLXArray<UInt64>`
- `FallbackKernelRunner` in `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/FallbackKernel.swift` is still a CPU scalar loop
- `LexPage.swift` still hard-codes `fastWinners: []`, so the branch is not actually extending the real M3 runtime
- `LexShell.swift` still uses an ad hoc page splitter rather than the deterministic frontend paging contract in `MicroSwiftFrontend`

## Desired End State
After the child beads are complete, the branch must satisfy the following high-level conditions:
1. The shipped lexer runtime is MLX-backed in `MicroSwiftTensorCore`.
2. The fallback family executes through a real MLX/Metal path, with a custom Metal kernel where needed.
3. The current CPU scalar fallback logic remains available only as a test/spec oracle.
4. The runtime extends the real M3 fast-path lexer rather than replacing it with a fallback-only scaffold.
5. Paging, overflow handling, packed transport, and cache reuse all match the M4 spec.
6. There is one canonical, fully automated proof path that demonstrates the fallback kernel actually executed on MLX/Metal. No human log reading is allowed.

## Dependency Graph Intent
The child beads are deliberately split so parallel work is possible after the initial runtime reconciliation:
- one foundation bead to put `m4` back on top of a real M3 runtime surface
- one MLX/runtime-bridge bead and one frontend-paging bead that can proceed in parallel
- one dedicated Metal-kernel bead once the MLX runtime bridge exists
- one integration bead to fold fast families and fallback into the same winner pipeline
- one cache/benchmark/observability bead after the kernel and pipeline exist
- one CLI/GPU-proof bead once the runtime is real
- one final hardening bead to ensure the test suite cannot pass on vacuous or host-only behavior

## Global Acceptance Criteria
- The production runtime path in `MicroSwiftTensorCore` uses MLX-backed runtime data and transport for the lexer hot path.
- `FallbackKernelRunner` no longer performs the DFA stepping on the CPU.
- The CPU `ScalarFallbackEvaluator` remains available for automated differential verification only.
- `v1-fallback` is a conservative extension of the real M3 runtime when fallback rules are absent.
- `localWindow` remains rejected for the runtime profile.
- There is an automated MLX/Metal execution proof that fails if the runtime silently regresses to CPU fallback stepping.

## Global Automated Proof Plan
The completed milestone must be closable with automated commands only. The child beads should converge toward a lane equivalent to:
1. `swift test`
2. `./dev verify`
3. `./dev mlx-smoke` or a successor command that emits machine-readable proof that the fallback kernel executed on MLX/Metal
4. Any new dedicated fallback smoke command added by the child beads, also emitting machine-readable JSON

No acceptance step may require manual inspection of profiler output, ad hoc REPL work, or human interpretation of free-form logs.

description:
## Problem Statement
The current `m4` branch is not actually extending the real Milestone 3 lexer runtime. The branch has fallback-specific types and tests, but `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPage.swift` still sets `fastWinners` to an empty array, and `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexShell.swift` is a minimal page splitter that does not match the richer runtime shape already implemented on `main`/`origin/m3`.

That means every later M4 task is currently sitting on top of a fallback-only scaffold instead of the real M3 page-oriented lexer pipeline that the spec says M4 is supposed to extend.

## Scope
Reconcile the `m4` branch with the actual M3 runtime surfaces before any MLX/Metal work proceeds.

This bead should either port or adapt the following classes of functionality into the `m4` branch while preserving the fallback-specific files that are already here:
- fast-family execution for literal and run rules
- packed token transport
- winner reduction and greedy selection integrated with real fast-family outputs
- keyword remap
- coverage and lexical error span generation
- token-tape style assembly or an equivalent deterministic page result story
- page-aware lex shell behavior that can later accept fallback winners cleanly

## Concrete Work Items
- Audit the gap between the current `m4` branch and `origin/m3` for `MicroSwiftTensorCore` runtime files.
- Port or adapt the missing M3 runtime files and tests into `m4`.
- Remove any stub logic that allows tests to pass while doing no real fast-path lexing.
- Reconcile naming conflicts so the branch has one coherent runtime API rather than two parallel lexer implementations.
- Ensure the fallback-specific types already on `m4` compile against the restored M3 runtime surfaces.
- Keep the runtime profile split (`v0` vs `v1-fallback`) intact while restoring real fast-family execution.

## Likely File Targets
- `Package.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPage.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexShell.swift`
- any missing or divergent M3 files needed for fast-path execution, such as loader, transport, keyword remap, coverage, overflow, packed-token, winner, selector, and page-shell surfaces
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/*` for ported/adapted M3 suites

## Non-Goals
- Do not implement the custom Metal kernel here.
- Do not convert the runtime to MLX-backed arrays yet unless required to keep the port coherent; that is handled in a later bead.
- Do not weaken the current fallback tests to accommodate stubbed fast-path behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `m4` contains a real fast-family lexer runtime equivalent in behavior to the existing M3 implementation.
- `LexPage.swift` no longer relies on `fastWinners: []`.
- `v0` lexing on non-fallback artifacts produces actual token streams, spans, and/or packed rows that correspond to real fast-path execution.
- Existing fallback-specific types and tests still compile after the reconciliation.
- The branch has one coherent production lex path rather than a fallback-only prototype living beside an unported M3 runtime.

## Automated Test Plan
The bead is only complete when all of the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. Restored/adapted M3 fast-path suites pass under `swift test`, including golden/integration coverage for fast-family tokenization.
3. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/V0CompatibilityTests.swift` passes for non-vacuous reasons: the assertions must be anchored by real outputs rather than both profiles returning empty results.
4. Any newly ported page-boundary, keyword-remap, coverage, and packed-row tests are exercised through `swift test` with no manual inspection.

## Closure Guardrail
This bead must not be closed if the branch can still satisfy compatibility tests by returning empty or stubbed winner sets. The automated tests added/ported here must make that impossible.

title: M4R9B: Port literal execution to shifted MLX tensor conjunctions
description:
## Problem Statement
`LiteralExecution` is the clearest example of the current architectural bottleneck: it still evaluates literals by walking host bytes position by position and building host candidate lengths. That work is embarrassingly data-parallel and should be one of the easiest parts of the lexer to move into an MLX graph, yet it is still on the CPU. As long as literals stay scalar, the highest-frequency punctuation and keyword-prefix tokens continue to burn host cycles and the rest of the page pipeline cannot stay resident on device.

## Scope
Port literal-family candidate generation from host loops to MLX tensor operations over the bucketed page bytes introduced by the device-ingestion bead. This bead owns shifted equality checks, conjunction across literal offsets, valid-position masking, and device-resident `candLen` emission for literal rules.

## Concrete Work Items
- Rework `LiteralExecution` so the production path accepts the device page contract rather than raw `[UInt8]` storage.
- Implement the core literal algorithm as shifted equality checks across the whole page, one tensor per literal offset, combined by boolean conjunction.
- Provide a deterministic shifted-view helper that pads or slices without changing shape and without reading invalid positions beyond the page bucket.
- Multiply or otherwise map the final boolean mask to the literal length so the resulting candidate-length tensor matches the existing semantics expected by downstream winner logic.
- Ensure literals of varying length still compose into a stable rule-axis representation for later reduction work; if literal bucketing by length is helpful, make that internal detail explicit and testable.
- Zero out starts that would extend past `validLen`, including cases where the literal prefix matches inside the valid prefix but later bytes would read only from padded tail.
- Preserve the existing deterministic metadata relationship between literal rule IDs, priority ranks, token kinds, and any later keyword remap logic.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LiteralExecution.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPageAPI.swift`
- any new tensor helper file extracted for shifted literal views
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LiteralExecutionTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/CandidateVerificationTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LexPageTests.swift` if page-local integration assertions are needed

## Non-Goals
- Do not leave a scalar per-byte loop in the production literal path and call it "vectorized" merely because the final result is wrapped in `MLXArray`.
- Do not accept early host extraction of literal candidate lengths to satisfy the old reducer.
- Do not weaken literal boundary tests to hide padding or off-by-one bugs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The production literal fast path evaluates matches through MLX tensor operations over the full bucketed page.
- The production path no longer performs scalar per-position literal matching on host arrays.
- Literal candidate lengths are emitted as device-resident tensors suitable for later fused reduction.
- Starts near `validLen` are masked correctly so padded tail bytes cannot create false literal matches.
- Existing literal semantics, including exact-match length and deterministic rule metadata association, are preserved.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when all of the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LiteralExecutionTests.swift`
3. Literal differential/property coverage in `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/CandidateVerificationTests.swift`, including randomized pages and a scalar oracle comparison.
4. Page-boundary or lex-page integration tests proving that literals spanning the end of `validLen` are rejected deterministically.
5. Any new literal-bucketing or shifted-view helper tests added under `swift test`.

## Closure Guardrail
Do not close this bead if the implementation still constructs host slices inside the hot loop or if the literal result reaches `WinnerReduction` by first converting back to `[UInt16]`. The literal family must become a real device-resident candidate producer.

title: M4R3: Replace the CPU FallbackKernelRunner with a custom Metal fallback executor behind the same API
description:
## Problem Statement
`Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/FallbackKernel.swift` currently implements fallback stepping as a pure Swift scalar loop over `[UInt16]`, with bitset updates performed on the CPU. That is the exact implementation gap called out by the spec review: the shipped path is a scalar reference implementation, not the required MLX/Metal fallback kernel.

## Scope
Keep the `FallbackKernelRunner` public surface stable if practical, but replace its internals so production fallback evaluation executes through MLX/Metal.

## Concrete Work Items
- Define the device/kernel ABI for the fallback runner, including:
  - input page class IDs
  - fixed max-width loop bound
  - 128-state bitset start masks
  - step tables
  - accept masks
  - per-rule metadata (`priorityRank`, `ruleID`, `tokenKindID`, `mode`)
  - output winner arrays (`fallbackLen`, `fallbackPriorityRank`, `fallbackRuleID`, `fallbackTokenKindID`, `fallbackMode`)
- Implement the bounded fallback stepping kernel as a custom Metal kernel, wrapped behind MLX if the MLX custom-kernel surface is usable, or behind a thin direct Metal wrapper if that is the only pragmatic route.
- Ensure the kernel uses one logical lane/thread per start position, a fixed-trip bounded loop, deterministic tie-breaking, and no inter-thread communication.
- Bind fallback tables as constant read-only buffers as required by `history/m4.md`.
- Preserve or improve the existing `FallbackObservability` metrics so the runtime can later prove the kernel executed.
- Keep `ScalarFallbackEvaluator` as the reference oracle in tests only.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/FallbackKernel.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/FallbackRuntime.swift`
- any new Metal shader source or wrapper file required by the implementation
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/FallbackMetrics.swift`
- `Package.swift` if resources or target configuration must change
- fallback differential/property tests

## Non-Goals
- Do not delete `ScalarFallbackEvaluator`; it is the spec oracle.
- Do not introduce a silent CPU fallback inside `FallbackKernelRunner` for the production path.
- Do not treat mutable device buffers as an acceptable substitute for constant table bindings.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `FallbackKernelRunner.evaluatePage(...)` executes the fallback stepping through the MLX/Metal production path rather than the current Swift scalar loop.
- The implementation preserves the current deterministic comparison order: longer match, then smaller `priorityRank`, then smaller `ruleID`.
- Fallback step tables, accept masks, and rule metadata are bound as constant read-only buffers or the nearest MLX abstraction that maps to that contract.
- The shipped runner no longer performs the DFA walk on the CPU.
- The existing scalar oracle and the new MLX/Metal runner agree across the current fallback fixtures and seeded random inputs.

## Automated Test Plan
All closure evidence must be automated:
1. `swift test`
2. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/FallbackKernelRunnerTests.swift`
3. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/DifferentialFallbackTests.swift`
4. `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/PropertyFallbackTests.swift`
5. An automated MLX/Metal smoke path added by this bead or a dependent bead must expose machine-readable proof that the fallback kernel backend executed; no manual profiler inspection is allowed.

## Closure Guardrail
This bead must not be closed if the implementation is still fundamentally the current Swift loop moved behind a different type name or wrapper. The production stepping logic must have left the CPU.

Required implementation detail:

title: M4R2: Add MLX-backed runtime bridge and transport types to MicroSwiftTensorCore
description:
## Problem Statement
The current `m4` branch still stores its production lexer runtime data in host-only arrays:
- `ArtifactRuntime.byteToClassLUT` is `[UInt8]`
- fallback tables are stored only in host collections
- `PageLexResult.packedRows` is `[UInt64]`

That contradicts the M4 spec, which explicitly calls for MLX-backed runtime constants and packed page-local result rows. Without this bridge, even a correct fallback kernel would still sit behind a host-oriented runtime contract.

## Scope
Convert `MicroSwiftTensorCore` from host-array runtime constants and page transport into MLX-backed runtime structures appropriate for the production path.

## Concrete Work Items
- Add `MLX` as a dependency of `MicroSwiftTensorCore` in `Package.swift`.
- Reintroduce any missing package dependencies needed to connect `MicroSwiftTensorCore` back to `MicroSwiftFrontend` if the reconciled runtime needs it.
- Change `ArtifactRuntime` so the production runtime constants use MLX-friendly representations, especially:
  - `byteToClassLUT: MLXArray<UInt16>` (or the exact MLX generic form used in the repo)
  - class-set runtime tables in a device-friendly representation
  - fallback runtime data staged for device upload / constant-buffer binding
- Change the production `PageLexResult` transport so packed rows are MLX-backed (`MLXArray<UInt64>` or an equivalent multiword MLX transport if required).
- Add explicit host extraction helpers used by tests and shell layers to inspect MLX-backed results deterministically.
- Ensure the bridge keeps deterministic padding and shape stability guarantees.

## Likely File Targets
- `Package.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/ArtifactRuntime.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/FallbackRuntime.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPage.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/PageLexResult`-equivalent surface if split out
- `Sources/MicroSwiftFrontend/*` only if dependency integration requires updates
- tests covering runtime bridge, packed rows, and extraction helpers

## Non-Goals
- Do not implement the Metal kernel in this bead.
- Do not keep a shadow host-only production runtime path just to make tests easier.
- Do not remove the scalar evaluator; it remains the test oracle.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `MicroSwiftTensorCore` directly depends on `MLX`.
- The production runtime uses MLX-backed constants for `byteToClassLUT` and packed row transport.
- The runtime bridge produces deterministic MLX-backed data structures suitable for later custom-kernel execution.
- Tests can inspect these MLX-backed values only through explicit extraction helpers or shell boundaries; there must be no hidden fallback to a host-only production path.
- The runtime bridge remains deterministic and enforces the existing fallback-capability invariants.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when all checks are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. `ArtifactRuntime` bridge tests assert shapes, dtypes, counts, state-cap invariants, and deterministic packing under `swift test`.
3. `LexPage`/transport tests assert deterministic zero-padding and stable host extraction from MLX-backed packed rows.
4. Any bridge-verification suites covering byte classification and class-set consistency run automatically under `swift test`.

## Closure Guardrail
Do not close this bead if the code merely wraps host arrays at the very edge while the production lexer hot path still fundamentally reasons over `[UInt8]` and `[UInt64]` as its canonical runtime storage.

M4R9D: Port prefixed execution to device stop-aware tensor operators
description:
## Problem Statement
The `prefixed` family is easy to forget because many artifacts only use it for line comments, but it is part of the real M3 fast path and it can break the entire compiled-graph story if left behind. A partially migrated fast path where literals and runs live on device but prefixed rules still scan host arrays would force mixed backends, early host extraction, or family-specific side channels through `LexPageAPI`. That would undercut the main objective of the migration.

## Scope
Port `prefixed` rule execution to the device-resident MLX fast-path contract. This bead owns prefix detection, body-class handling, optional stop-class handling, maximal page-local extent computation, and device-resident `candLen` emission for prefixed rules.

## Concrete Work Items
- Reuse or adapt the literal-shift machinery to detect the fixed prefix of each prefixed rule over the bucketed page bytes.
- Recover body and optional stop-class membership from artifact-driven class-set tables on device.
- Implement the page-local extent computation for the prefixed body, including the stop-aware case described in the M3 spec and the bounded/no-stop case when `stopClassSetID` is absent.
- If a reverse next-stop helper or similar page-local structure is the cleanest formulation, keep it shape-stable and purely page-local.
- Zero out candidates that would begin in valid bytes but rely on padded tail bytes for either prefix or body extent.
- Ensure the result plugs into the shared candidate-tensor representation expected by the reduction bead.
- Preserve the current semantics for common comment-like rules while keeping the implementation generic over artifact data.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/PrefixedExecution.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPageAPI.swift`
- any shared tensor-helper file that houses stop-aware page-local helper logic
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/CandidateVerificationTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/PageBoundaryTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LexPageTests.swift`
- relevant golden suites that cover comment-style prefixed rules

## Non-Goals
- Do not special-case line comments so aggressively that the runtime stops being generic over artifact-emitted prefixed rules.
- Do not pull prefixed candidates back to host memory just because the old reducer still expects host arrays.
- Do not relax page-local semantics by reading across page boundaries or peeking into the next page.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Prefixed rules execute through the device-resident fast-path contract rather than host scanning.
- Prefix detection, body extent, and optional stop handling all remain page-local and deterministic.
- Padded tail bytes and page boundaries cannot create false prefix matches or over-extended prefixed runs.
- Prefixed candidates are emitted as device-resident tensors ready for the fused reduction stage.
- The implementation remains generic over artifact-emitted prefixes and class sets.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when all of the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. Prefixed differential coverage in `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/CandidateVerificationTests.swift`
3. Page-boundary tests that specifically cover stop-aware prefixed rules, including comment-like rules ending at newline or end-of-page.
4. Lex-page or golden tests showing prefixed tokens integrate correctly with the rest of the fast path.
5. Any new helper tests for reverse-stop or next-stop structures under `swift test`.

## Closure Guardrail
Do not close this bead if `prefixed` execution still depends on host scanning or if correctness relies on reading beyond the current page. The prefixed family must become a true page-local device candidate producer.

Any tests that exercise MLX tensor ops requiring GPU shaders MUST be run with xcodebuild (e.g. xcodebuild test -scheme MicroSwift -destination 'platform=macOS'). Use swift build and swift test only for compilation checks and non-GPU tests. Design code so that the core logic is testable without GPU, but integration/smoke tests that touch real MLX execution should be validated via xcodebuild.

description:
Problem: `PageLexResult` is host-biased. It stores packed rows as host arrays immediately, and its MLX-facing accessor recreates a device tensor from that host storage. This prevents the final transport boundary from remaining lazy and makes future device-resident paths more expensive than necessary.

Required implementation detail:
- Refactor `PageLexResult` so it can retain packed rows in device form until a host consumer explicitly requests host extraction.
- Keep the public result semantics intact: equality, token unpacking, and existing callers should continue to work.
- Avoid changing unrelated result fields unless required to support lazy transport.
- Make extraction boundaries explicit so tests can distinguish benchmark/device execution from final host materialization.

title: M4R9A: Establish bucket-stable device page ingestion and no-early-egress fast-path inputs
description:
## Problem Statement
The new compiled-fast-path plan starts with one hard requirement: every page must enter the runtime as a fixed-shape device tensor and stay there. The current production surface still allows `LexingShell`, `LexPageAPI`, and family executors to traffic in host `[UInt8]` page bytes and host-derived intermediates. Even with bucketed paging, that leaves the hot path vulnerable to repeated uploads, ad hoc host/device conversions, and shape instability that will sabotage `MLX.compile` reuse.

If this bead is not done first, every later family-migration bead risks building against the wrong contract.

## Scope
Define the production fast-path page-input contract for compiled MLX execution. This bead owns the one-shot upload of a bucket-padded page, the device-resident representation of `validLen`-aware bytes and class IDs, and the explicit rule that no host extraction is allowed before final packed transport rows are ready.

## Concrete Work Items
- Introduce or adapt a page-local runtime type that represents one compiled page invocation, for example a `CompiledPageInput`, `BucketedPageTensor`, or equivalent surface that is clearly the canonical fast-path input.
- Ensure every production page is padded to exactly `PageBucket.byteCapacity` using a deterministic neutral byte that cannot create false fast-family matches.
- Upload the padded page exactly once as `MLXArray` byte storage with a stable dtype and rank for the selected bucket.
- Carry `validLen`, `baseOffset`, and any required valid-position mask explicitly so the graph can ignore padded tail bytes without changing shape.
- Derive byte classification on device from the runtime LUT so family beads can consume `classIDs` without reconstructing host-side views of the page.
- Provide any shared tensor helpers needed by later beads for shifted views, valid-range masking, and deterministic tail zeroing, but do not push family-specific logic into this foundation bead.
- Make the extraction boundary explicit: tests and shell layers may inspect results through dedicated helpers, but the production fast path must not pull bytes or candidates back to host memory before final packed rows exist.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexingShell.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPageAPI.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/PageBucket.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/ArtifactRuntime.swift`
- a new page-input helper such as `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/CompiledPageInput.swift` if that separation makes the contract clearer
- `Sources/MicroSwiftFrontend/PreparedSource.swift` only if the integration boundary needs a small adaptation
- tests covering deterministic padding, valid-length masking, and class-ID derivation

## Non-Goals
- Do not port any specific rule family in this bead.
- Do not allow a shadow host-array fast-path input just because some tests are easier to write that way.
- Do not make page shape stability depend on runtime data values or rule contents.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The production fast path consumes bucket-padded `MLXArray` page bytes as its canonical per-page input.
- The padded page shape is exactly the selected bucket capacity and is deterministic for repeated runs over the same page.
- `classIDs` or the equivalent byte-classification view are derived on device from runtime tables and are available to downstream MLX family executors without re-materializing host arrays.
- The neutral padding byte and valid-length masking guarantee that bytes beyond `validLen` cannot create literal, run, or prefixed matches.
- The codebase has an explicit extraction boundary for host inspection, and production execution does not cross that boundary before final packed rows are constructed.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when all of the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. New or updated page-input tests assert exact bucket-sized tensor shapes, dtypes, and deterministic padding under `swift test`.
3. Differential tests confirm device-side byte classification matches the scalar oracle for representative artifacts and randomized page contents.
4. Page-boundary suites assert that padded tail bytes beyond `validLen` never contribute false matches or shifted-window artifacts.
5. Any extraction helper added by this bead is covered by tests that prove it is invoked only at explicit boundaries rather than implicitly inside production execution.

## Closure Guardrail
Do not close this bead if the code still treats host `[UInt8]` page bytes as the canonical production input and merely wraps them in `MLXArray` inside individual helper calls. The canonical page contract itself must become device-resident and shape-stable.
notes: IMPORTANT BUILD NOTE: Metal shaders cannot be compiled via swift package manager (swift build/swift test). Any tests that exercise MLX tensor ops requiring GPU shaders MUST be run with xcodebuild (e.g. xcodebuild test -scheme MicroSwift -destination 'platform=macOS'). Use swift build and swift test only for compilation checks and non-GPU tests. Design code so that the core logic is testable without GPU, but integration/smoke tests that touch real MLX execution should be validated via xcodebuild.
close_reason: Completed - CompiledPageInput contract with bucket-stable device tensors

title: M4R7: Add a fully automated CLI and smoke-proof lane that demonstrates the fallback kernel executed on MLX/Metal
description:
## Problem Statement
The repository already has `mlx-smoke`, but today it only proves that a trivial MLX operation can execute. It does not prove that the production fallback runtime executed the actual bounded fallback kernel on MLX/Metal. The milestone needs a canonical automated proof path, not a human promise.

## Scope
Create or extend a CLI/smoke surface so a single automated command can build the binary via the supported Xcode path, run a fallback-heavy workload through the production runtime, and emit machine-readable evidence that the fallback kernel executed on MLX/Metal.

## Concrete Work Items
- Decide whether to extend `mlx-smoke` or add a new dedicated fallback smoke command.
- Feed a deterministic fallback-heavy artifact and input through the production runtime.
- Emit machine-readable fields that prove the production device path was used, for example:
  - runtime profile
  - backend identifier
  - kernel or pipeline identifier
  - fallback positions entered
  - fallback kernel execution count or a boolean equivalent
  - artifact hash or fixture identifier
- Wire the command into the existing Xcode/`Scripts/mlx-run` command-line execution story.
- Update CLI snapshots/tests if the command surface changes.
- Ensure the command fails automatically if the runtime silently regresses to host-only fallback stepping.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftCLI/Commands/MLXSmoke.swift` or a new CLI command file
- `Sources/MicroSwiftCLI/MicroSwiftCLI.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftCLI/Dependencies/DependencyClients.swift` if runtime injection needs expansion
- `Scripts/dev`
- `Scripts/mlx-run` only if invocation shape changes
- CLI tests and snapshots

## Non-Goals
- Do not rely on a human to inspect free-form logs.
- Do not make “GPU path probably ran” an implicit assumption.
- Do not hide a CPU regression behind a command that still returns `ok`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- There is one canonical command-line proof path for the fallback MLX/Metal runtime.
- The command is machine-readable and suitable for automation from a fresh checkout.
- The emitted output contains enough information for tests to prove the fallback kernel executed on the production device path.
- The command fails automatically if the runtime silently falls back to host-only stepping.
- CLI tests and snapshots cover the output shape if the user-facing surface changes.

## Automated Test Plan
The bead is complete only when all of the following are automated:
1. `swift test`
2. `Tests/MicroSwiftCLITests/*` covering the smoke command output
3. `./dev mlx-smoke` or the successor smoke command returns machine-readable output proving fallback MLX/Metal execution
4. `./dev verify` includes the same automated proof lane, or invokes a dedicated automated wrapper that fails on regression
5. the Xcode/`Scripts/mlx-run` invocation path is exercised without manual setup beyond the documented repo commands

## Closure Guardrail
This bead must not be closed if the smoke lane can still report success while the production fallback runner executes on the CPU.

description:
## Problem Statement
The current `m4` branch already has useful cache and benchmark scaffolding, but it still assumes the cached object is the current CPU `FallbackKernelRunner`. The benchmark lane and cache-hit accounting therefore do not yet prove anything about compiled MLX/Metal resources or constant-buffer reuse.

## Scope

## Concrete Work Items
- Update `KernelCache` so entries represent the real compiled/runtime resources needed by the fallback kernel:
  - MLX compiled wrapper function and/or Metal pipeline state

- Preserve deterministic cache keys such as `(deviceID, artifactHash, pageBucket, inputDType)`.
- Update `FallbackObservability` and `FallbackBenchmark` so they can prove:
  - cache miss vs hit behavior
  - cold vs warm reuse

## Likely File Targets

- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/FallbackBenchmark.swift`

## Non-Goals

## Acceptance Criteria
- Cache entries represent real MLX/Metal runtime resources, not merely the current CPU runner object.
- Cache miss/hit behavior is deterministic and exposed through machine-readable structured records.

- Warm runs reuse the same kernel resources for the same cache key.

## Automated Test Plan
All closure evidence must be automated:

M4R9C: Port classRun/headTail execution to device edge-detection and run-length primitives
description:
## Problem Statement
The `classRun` and `headTail` families are currently expressed in the repo as sequential logic over host arrays, with the runtime discovering starts and maximal ends by walking the page byte by byte. That is the opposite of what a tensor compiler wants. These rules should instead be framed as class-membership masks, edge detection, and run-length recovery. Until that happens, identifier and integer tokenization remain CPU-bound and the post-literal fast path never becomes a true MLX computation graph.

## Scope
Port `classRun` and `headTail` candidate generation to device-resident MLX execution. This bead owns class-set membership recovery from runtime tables, start/end edge detection, min-length handling, run-length recovery, and device emission of candidate lengths for both families. If inclusive scans or stream compaction are too awkward in stock MLX for this repo's performance and clarity targets, this bead may introduce a very small dedicated Metal helper kernel for the run-length primitive, but the production API must still present a clean MLX-oriented device path.

## Concrete Work Items
- Map page bytes or class IDs to class-set membership tensors directly from artifact-emitted runtime tables; do not hardcode assumptions like "digit" or "identifier continue" into the runtime.
- For `classRun`, compute the membership mask, start mask, end mask, and maximal run lengths using a device-resident primitive that stays shape-stable for the bucket.
- For `headTail`, compute the head mask, tail membership mask, and maximal continuation lengths so identifier-style rules preserve the current semantics.
- Implement min-length filtering and zeroing on device.
- Decide whether the run-length primitive should be expressed as MLX scan math, a tiny custom Metal helper, or a hybrid surface. The decision must preserve deterministic semantics, page locality, and automated testability.
- Ensure padded tail bytes beyond `validLen` never participate in class masks, start masks, or end masks.
- Expose outputs in the shared candidate-tensor form expected by the later reduction bead.

## Likely File Targets
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/ClassRunExecution.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/HeadTailExecution.swift`
- `Sources/MicroSwiftTensorCore/LexPageAPI.swift`
- a new helper file if run-length recovery or class-mask plumbing deserves its own surface
- an optional tiny Metal/helper wrapper if pure MLX scan composition is not the cleanest solution
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/CandidateVerificationTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/LexPageTests.swift`
- `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/PageBoundaryTests.swift`

## Non-Goals
- Do not reintroduce host `while` loops in the production path under the guise of a helper function.

- `classRun` and `headTail` candidate lengths are computed through device-resident execution over MLX-friendly tensors.
- The production path no longer walks the page byte by byte on the CPU for these rule families.
- Class-set semantics come from artifact runtime tables, not hardcoded character logic.
- Min-length filtering, padded-tail masking, and maximal-run semantics all match the scalar oracle.
- The resulting candidate tensors are ready for fused rule-axis reduction without first becoming host arrays.

## Automated Test Plan
This bead is complete only when all of the following are automated and passing:
1. `swift test`
2. The `classRun` and `headTail` differential suites in `Tests/MicroSwiftTensorCoreTests/CandidateVerificationTests.swift`
3. Randomized scalar-oracle agreement tests that vary page length, bucket, class-set membership, and min-length constraints.
4. Page-boundary tests proving starts and maximal ends do not leak into padded tail bytes.
5. If a tiny custom kernel is introduced, backend-selection and result-shape tests that run automatically under `swift test` and fail on any silent CPU fallback.

## Closure Guardrail
Do not close this bead if `classRun` or `headTail` still depend on host loops for start/end discovery or run-length recovery. A wrapper around the existing scalar algorithm is not enough.

## Problem Statement
The repository has an M4 remediation epic and a first wave of remediation beads, but the newly proposed migration from host-array fast-path execution to compiled MLX page graphs is not yet represented as an explicit, parallelizable dependency graph in beads. Without that planning layer, the work can drift into overlapping implementation beads, vague closure criteria, or manual proof steps.

## Scope
Create the bead graph for the compiled MLX fast-path migration, including the parent epic, detailed child beads, and the dependency edges that preserve parallelism while keeping the architectural sequencing honest.

## Concrete Work Items
- Audit the current M4 remediation beads and identify where the compiled fast-path migration is already covered versus still missing.
- Create a dedicated compiled-fast-path epic under the existing M4 remediation epic.
- Create child beads with detailed problem statements, scope, work items, non-goals, acceptance criteria, automated test plans, and closure guardrails.
- Wire dependencies to maximize parallelism without breaking the required ordering around page ingestion, family execution, reduction, transport, and compiled-graph caching.
- Sync the bead store so the resulting graph is checked into git.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The repo contains a new epic and child bead graph covering the compiled MLX fast-path migration.
- The dependency graph exposes the intended parallel branches explicitly.
- Every newly created bead has detailed automated acceptance criteria and a non-manual test plan.

## Automated Test Plan
This planning bead is complete only when the following are automated and passing:

## Closure Guardrail
